steps.title: Spot the defining self-doubt
steps.content: The strongest clue is Camillo's parenthetical qualifier: “or I o'erween [presume] to think so.” He uses this phrase to express doubt about whether he can truly help the Sicilian king with his sorrows.

steps.title: Match the uncertainty to the choices
steps.content:
Choice C correctly identifies what the underlined portion conveys: Camillo recognizes that the benefit he claims is merely presumed, not assured.

The other choices either misidentify what the underlined portion addresses or miss how the self-doubt weakens rather than strengthens the force of his claim.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
